biochemistry.title: Biochemistry
biochemistry.overview: Biochemistry sits at the heart of modern life science, combining chemistry and biology to explain how living systems work at the molecular level. You will study proteins, nucleic acids, lipids and carbohydrates — the molecules of life — and learn how they interact to drive metabolism, cell signalling, gene expression and disease. The discipline is fundamental to drug discovery, medical diagnostics, agricultural biotechnology and synthetic biology. UK biochemistry departments produce world-leading research and have strong links with the NHS, pharmaceutical companies such as AstraZeneca, GSK and Pfizer, and national institutes including the Francis Crick Institute and the Wellcome Sanger Institute.

modules: Advanced Topics: CRISPR and Gene Editing

skills: Recombinant DNA technology and gene cloning
Protein expression, purification and characterisation (SDS-PAGE, Western blot, HPLC, mass spectrometry)
Scientific writing, data analysis and statistical interpretation
Laboratory safety and GLP (Good Laboratory Practice)

professionalBodies.name: Royal Society of Biology (RSB)
professionalBodies.benefit: Accredited degrees lead to Graduate Member (GradRSB) status and a pathway to Chartered Biologist (CBiol).

professionalBodies.name: Royal Society of Chemistry (RSC)
professionalBodies.benefit: Chemistry-heavy programmes may carry RSC accreditation, supporting Graduate Chemist status.

professionalBodies.name: Institute of Biomedical Science (IBMS)
professionalBodies.benefit: Some programmes are IBMS-affiliated, supporting entry into NHS clinical biochemistry and pathology careers.

biochemistry.employerPerspective: The pharmaceutical and biotechnology industries recruit heavily from UK biochemistry programmes. AstraZeneca and GSK run dedicated graduate schemes targeting biochemistry and molecular biology graduates; the NHS recruits into Clinical Scientist posts for accredited degrees; and the growing UK biotech sector — particularly around Cambridge, Oxford and London — creates strong demand for graduates with practical wet-lab and analytical skills.
biochemistry.entryRequirements: Typical offer: AAB–A*AA at A-level depending on university. Chemistry is required at virtually all institutions; Biology is required or strongly recommended at most. Mathematics is advantageous for enzyme kinetics and bioinformatics. GCSE Maths at grade 6/B or above is commonly required.
biochemistry.furtherStudy: MSc Biochemistry, MSc Molecular Medicine, MSc Drug Discovery, PhD Biochemistry / Molecular Biology, MSc Bioinformatics, MSc Biotechnology.
isItForYou: Study this if you find the molecular mechanisms of life fascinating and enjoy experimental laboratory work alongside scientific reasoning.
Study this if you want maximum flexibility — biochemistry graduates enter medicine (via Graduate Entry Medicine), pharmacy, clinical science, data science and management consulting.
Study this if you want to contribute to drug discovery, disease research or biotechnology — areas with growing global demand.
Think twice if you dislike chemistry: a strong grasp of organic and physical chemistry underpins most of the degree, especially in Years 1 and 2.
Think twice if you expect a purely theoretical degree — substantial laboratory time is a core part of all accredited programmes.

chemistry.title: Chemistry
chemistry.overview: Chemistry is the central science, bridging physics and biology and underpinning much of modern technology. A chemistry degree covers organic, inorganic and physical chemistry, with increasing opportunities to specialise in computational chemistry, green chemistry, materials science and medicinal chemistry. UK chemistry is internationally renowned — UK chemists have won more Nobel Prizes per capita than virtually any other nation — and the sector employs over 500,000 people across pharmaceuticals, materials, energy and food. The MChem integrated Masters is now the standard qualification for professional chemists in the UK.

modules: Physical Chemistry II (Quantum Mechanics and Statistical Mechanics)
Advanced Analytical Techniques (NMR, Mass Spec, X-ray)

skills: NMR spectroscopy, mass spectrometry and IR analysis
Scientific literature searching and critical evaluation
Quantitative data analysis and error propagation
Laboratory safety and COSHH risk assessment

professionalBodies.name: Royal Society of Chemistry (RSC)
professionalBodies.benefit: An MChem from an RSC-accredited institution leads to Chartered Chemist (CChem) status with subsequent experience. Accreditation is held by the great majority of Russell Group chemistry departments.

professionalBodies.name: Royal Society of Biology (RSB)
professionalBodies.benefit: Relevant for chemical biology and green chemistry graduates pursuing biological science careers.

chemistry.employerPerspective: Chemistry graduates are among the most versatile science graduates in the UK job market. The pharmaceutical industry recruits heavily through Year-in-Industry and graduate schemes; Johnson Matthey, INEOS and Shell run major graduate programmes specifically targeting MChem graduates. Patent attorney firms seek chemistry graduates for their scientific rigour.
chemistry.entryRequirements: Typical offer: AAB–A*AA at A-level. Chemistry is always required; Mathematics is required or strongly recommended at most universities and essential at Oxford, Cambridge, Imperial and Warwick. Further Mathematics is advantageous at the most competitive institutions.
chemistry.furtherStudy: PhD Chemistry, MSc Green Chemistry, MSc Medicinal Chemistry, MSc Analytical Science, MSc Materials Chemistry.
isItForYou: Study this if you love problem-solving at the molecular level and enjoy both theoretical reasoning and practical laboratory work.
Study this if you want exceptional career breadth — chemists work in pharmaceuticals, materials, energy, food, finance (as patent attorneys) and policy.
Study this if you are interested in sustainability — green chemistry and the circular economy are major growth areas.
Think twice if you find rigorous mathematical treatment difficult — physical chemistry and computational chemistry require comfort with calculus and linear algebra.
Think twice if you expect to avoid the laboratory — chemistry is lab-intensive; the MChem in particular requires extended experimental research.

natSci.title: Natural Sciences
natSci.overview: Natural Sciences programmes offer a broad entry into science, letting students combine chemistry, biology, physics, mathematics and earth sciences before specialising. Cambridge's Natural Sciences Tripos is the most prestigious science degree in the UK, but Durham, UCL, Bath, Exeter and York also offer well-regarded variants. The format suits students passionate about multiple sciences who are not ready to commit to one discipline at 18. Students emerge as chemists, biochemists, physicists or biologists — often indistinguishable in professional terms from single-subject graduates.

modules: Two or three science disciplines (e.g. Chemistry + Biology + Maths)
One or two disciplines at advanced level
Single discipline at Honours level (e.g. Biochemistry, Chemistry)

yearByYear.focus: Masters Research (MSci / Part III at Cambridge)
modules: Cutting-edge research at Part III level

skills: Laboratory skills across chemistry and biology
Intellectual flexibility valued by employers beyond science

professionalBodies.name: Royal Society of Chemistry (RSC)
professionalBodies.benefit: Chemistry-stream Natural Sciences graduates from RSC-accredited programmes may qualify for Graduate Chemist status.

professionalBodies.name: Royal Society of Biology (RSB)
professionalBodies.benefit: Biology-stream graduates may qualify for Graduate Member status and eventually Chartered Biologist.

natSci.employerPerspective: Cambridge Natural Sciences graduates are among the most sought-after in the UK job market — the degree's breadth combined with deep final-year specialisation is highly valued by management consultancies, investment banks, tech giants and pharmaceutical companies.
natSci.entryRequirements: Typical offer: A*A*A (Cambridge) to ABB (other institutions). Chemistry and/or Biology required; Mathematics strongly recommended for physical science pathways. Cambridge requires the Natural Sciences Admissions Assessment (NSAA).
natSci.furtherStudy: PhD in the Year 3 specialism, MSc depending on specialism. Many Cambridge Natural Scientists proceed directly to Part III (MSci) or to PhD without a separate Masters.
isItForYou: Study this if you genuinely can't choose between Chemistry, Biology and Physics — and want to keep options open into Year 2.
Study this if you aspire to Cambridge — it is the only way to study biochemistry or chemistry at Cambridge at undergraduate level.
Study this if you want the intellectual credibility of a broad science education combined with deep final-year research.
Think twice if you already know you want to be a biochemist or chemist — a single-subject MBiochem or MChem provides more specialist depth.
Think twice if you dislike managing breadth — the early years demand strong performance across multiple subjects simultaneously.

biomedSci.title: Biomedical Sciences
biomedSci.overview: Biomedical Sciences sits at the interface of biology, chemistry and medicine, focusing on the science of human health and disease. Students study human physiology, molecular biology, immunology, pathology and pharmacology. Top programmes at UCL, King's, Edinburgh and Imperial carry IBMS accreditation, supporting direct entry into NHS Clinical Scientist training.
skills: Clinical laboratory techniques (haematology, clinical biochemistry, histopathology)
Microscopy, flow cytometry and cell biology
Patient data interpretation and medical statistics

professionalBodies.name: Institute of Biomedical Science (IBMS)
professionalBodies.benefit: IBMS-accredited degrees are the gateway to NHS Clinical Scientist Scientist-in-Training and Biomedical Scientist HCPC registration.

professionalBodies.name: Health and Care Professions Council (HCPC)
professionalBodies.benefit: HCPC registration as a Biomedical Scientist is required to work in NHS diagnostic laboratories.

biomedSci.employerPerspective: The NHS is the largest single employer of biomedical science graduates; IBMS-accredited degrees give direct entry to the Clinical Scientist training route. The pharmaceutical industry recruits through clinical trials roles; Roche, Thermo Fisher and Sysmex specifically target biomedical science graduates.
biomedSci.entryRequirements: Typical offer: ABB–AAA at A-level. Biology required at most institutions; Chemistry required or strongly recommended. Top programmes (UCL, Imperial, King's, Edinburgh) require AAA or higher.
biomedSci.furtherStudy: MSc Biomedical Science, MSc Clinical Research, Graduate Entry Medicine (4-year MBChB/MBBS), MSc Genetic Counselling, MSc Neuroscience.
isItForYou: Study this if you want to work at the interface of science and medicine — particularly in NHS clinical laboratories or clinical research.
Study this if you find human disease fascinating but don't want to study medicine directly.
Study this if you are interested in diagnostics, genomics or personalised medicine.
Think twice if you want to practise as a doctor — biomedical sciences requires a separate Graduate Entry Medicine application.
Think twice if you dislike the clinical laboratory environment — the degree and its career paths are heavily laboratory-focused.

pharmacology.title: Pharmacology
pharmacology.overview: Pharmacology is the science of drugs — how they interact with the body's biological targets, how they are absorbed, distributed and eliminated, and how they produce therapeutic and adverse effects. It bridges biochemistry, physiology and medicine, and is the scientific foundation of the global pharmaceutical industry. UK programmes are closely linked to the pharmaceutical sector; the British Pharmacological Society (BPS) accredits programmes at leading universities.
skills: Receptor binding assays and functional pharmacology
Cell culture and in vitro pharmacological testing

professionalBodies.name: British Pharmacological Society (BPS)
professionalBodies.benefit: BPS-accredited degrees are the gold standard for UK pharmacology, supporting professional recognition and networking.

pharmacology.employerPerspective: The pharmaceutical industry recruits pharmacology graduates at scale through graduate schemes and year-in-industry programmes. Regulatory agencies including the MHRA also recruit pharmacology graduates to assess drug submissions.
pharmacology.entryRequirements: Typical offer: ABB–AAA at A-level. Chemistry and Biology both required at most universities. A-level Maths advantageous for pharmacokinetics.
pharmacology.furtherStudy: PhD Pharmacology, MSc Drug Discovery and Development, MSc Clinical Pharmacology, MSc Toxicology.
isItForYou: Study this if you are fascinated by how drugs work and want to understand disease at molecular, cellular and whole-body levels.
Study this if you want direct routes into the pharmaceutical industry, NHS clinical pharmacology or drug regulation.
Study this if you are considering medicine but also open to a science career path.
Think twice if you are primarily interested in chemistry — Medicinal Chemistry provides more synthetic depth.
Think twice if you expect to dispense or prescribe drugs — pharmacology is the science of drugs, not a pharmacy qualification.

molBiol.title: Molecular Biology
molBiol.overview: Molecular Biology investigates the molecular mechanisms underpinning living processes: DNA replication, gene transcription and translation, protein synthesis, cell division and signal transduction. Modern molecular biology has been transformed by next-generation sequencing, CRISPR gene editing, single-cell analysis and cryo-electron microscopy. Graduates are in demand across academic research, clinical genomics, biotechnology and synthetic biology.

professionalBodies.name: Royal Society of Biology (RSB)
professionalBodies.benefit: Accredited degrees support Graduate Member status and pathway to Chartered Biologist.

molBiol.employerPerspective: The UK genomics sector is growing rapidly. Molecular biology graduates are in high demand for sequencing, bioinformatics and gene therapy roles. CRISPR and RNA therapeutic companies are creating new graduate entry positions.
molBiol.entryRequirements: Typical offer: ABB–A*AA at A-level. Biology and Chemistry both required or strongly recommended. Mathematics advantageous for bioinformatics-heavy programmes.
molBiol.furtherStudy: PhD Molecular Biology / Biochemistry, MSc Bioinformatics, MSc Genomic Medicine, MSc Synthetic Biology.
isItForYou: Study this if you are captivated by gene editing, genomics and the molecular machinery of life.
Study this if you want to work in the UK genomics revolution — both the NHS and biotech sectors are creating significant demand.
Study this if you enjoy programming and data analysis alongside wet-lab work.
Think twice if primarily interested in drug development — pharmacology or medicinal chemistry provides a more directly pharmaceutical path.
Think twice if you dislike biology at the molecular level — the degree demands engagement with complex cellular processes in significant depth.

medicinalChem.title: Medicinal Chemistry
medicinalChem.overview: Medicinal Chemistry sits at the intersection of organic chemistry and pharmacology, focusing on the design, synthesis and optimisation of drug molecules. It is the scientific engine of drug discovery — from target identification through lead optimisation to candidate selection. UK programmes are closely linked to the pharmaceutical industry, with Bath, Nottingham and Manchester particularly renowned for industrial placement opportunities.

professionalBodies.name: Royal Society of Chemistry (RSC)
professionalBodies.benefit: MChem programmes carry RSC accreditation, supporting Graduate Chemist status and pathway to CChem.

medicinalChem.employerPerspective: AstraZeneca, GSK and Pfizer operate year-in-industry and graduate programmes specifically targeting medicinal chemistry students. AI-driven drug discovery companies (Exscientia, Recursion) are creating new roles combining synthetic chemistry with data science.
medicinalChem.entryRequirements: Typical offer: AAB–A*AA at A-level. Chemistry always required; Mathematics required or strongly recommended; Biology advantageous.
medicinalChem.furtherStudy: PhD Medicinal Chemistry, MSc Drug Discovery, MSc Computational Chemistry, MSc Pharmaceutical Sciences.
isItForYou: Study this if you love organic chemistry and want to apply it directly to making drugs that treat human disease.
Study this if you want the strongest possible direct pathway into pharmaceutical industry research roles.
Study this if you are interested in computational approaches — AI drug design is a rapidly growing specialty.
Think twice if you are primarily interested in biology — pharmacology or biomedical sciences provides more biological depth.
Think twice if you dislike lab-intensive synthesis — medicinal chemistry involves sustained, detailed synthetic work.

genetics.title: Genetics
genetics.overview: Genetics studies heredity, variation and the molecular mechanisms of inheritance across all forms of life. The sequencing of thousands of genomes, CRISPR gene editing and single-cell transcriptomics have transformed the field. UK genetics departments are internationally renowned; the Wellcome Sanger Institute and MRC Human Genetics Unit are global leaders. Genetics graduates are in growing demand across NHS Genomic Medicine, agricultural biotechnology, forensic science and pharmaceutical research.

modules: Cell Biology and the Cell Cycle

skills: PCR, gel electrophoresis and DNA sequencing
Ethics of genetic testing and genetic data

professionalBodies.name: Royal Society of Biology (RSB)
professionalBodies.benefit: Accredited genetics degrees lead to Graduate Member status and support Chartered Biologist qualification.

professionalBodies.name: Association for Clinical Genomic Science (ACGS)
professionalBodies.benefit: ACGS recognition supports NHS Genomic Medicine Service career pathways.

genetics.employerPerspective: The NHS Genomic Medicine Service is creating a significant cohort of clinical genomics scientist roles. Genomics England and Illumina's UK operations are major employers. Agricultural biotech companies and forensic science services also recruit genetics graduates.
genetics.entryRequirements: Typical offer: ABB–A*A*A at A-level. Biology almost always required; Chemistry required or strongly recommended. Mathematics advantageous for bioinformatics and statistical genetics.
genetics.furtherStudy: MSc Genomic Medicine, MSc Bioinformatics, MSc Genetic Counselling, PhD Genetics / Genomics.
isItForYou: Study this if you are fascinated by the code of life — how genes are organised, regulated and how mutations cause disease.
Study this if you want a pathway into the rapidly growing NHS Genomic Medicine Service or genomics research.
Study this if you enjoy data and computation as much as laboratory work — modern genetics is increasingly computational.
Think twice if primarily interested in chemistry — biochemistry or medicinal chemistry provides better chemical depth.
Think twice if expecting to become a doctor — genetics alone does not provide clinical practice qualifications.

microbiology.title: Microbiology
microbiology.duration: 3 years (BSc) / 4 years (Scottish Honours or sandwich placement)
microbiology.overview: Microbiology studies microorganisms — bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites and archaea — and their impact on human health, agriculture, the environment and industry. The COVID-19 pandemic and the antimicrobial resistance (AMR) crisis have made the discipline more important than ever. UK microbiology programmes are supported by world-class research; the Wellcome Centre for Anti-Infectives Research, the Centre for Virus Research (Glasgow) and the MRC Laboratory of Molecular Biology are global leaders.

professionalBodies.name: Microbiology Society
professionalBodies.benefit: Graduate membership provides access to funding and networking; full membership leads to Chartered Microbiologist (CMariol) status.

professionalBodies.name: Institute of Biomedical Science (IBMS)
professionalBodies.benefit: Graduates entering NHS clinical microbiology may pursue IBMS registration.

microbiology.employerPerspective: The NHS is a major employer through clinical microbiology and infection control services. The UKHSA recruits microbiologists for surveillance and outbreak response. AstraZeneca and GSK's vaccine divisions have expanded their UK presence following COVID-19.
microbiology.entryRequirements: Typical offer: BBB–AAA at A-level. Biology always required; Chemistry required or strongly recommended at competitive institutions.
microbiology.furtherStudy: PhD Microbiology / Infection Biology, MSc Medical Microbiology, MSc Clinical Microbiology, MSc Global Health, MSc Antimicrobial Resistance.
isItForYou: Study this if you are passionate about infectious disease, pandemic preparedness and the invisible world of microorganisms.
Study this if you want to work in clinical microbiology, public health or vaccine science.
Study this if the AMR crisis motivates you — one of the defining global health challenges of the coming decades.
Think twice if more interested in molecules than organisms — biochemistry or medicinal chemistry might be a better fit.
Think twice if you dislike biosafety-intensive laboratories — clinical microbiology involves working with live pathogens.

biochemIndustry.title: Biochemistry with Industrial Placement
biochemIndustry.duration: 4 years (BSc or MBiochem with integrated placement year)
biochemIndustry.overview: Biochemistry with Industrial Placement combines a full biochemistry degree with a paid, structured year working in a pharmaceutical, biotechnology or healthcare company — usually taken between Years 2 and 3. This dramatically improves graduate employment prospects. Bath, Surrey, Warwick and Nottingham are particularly renowned for placement quality. Placement salaries of £18,000–£25,000 also meaningfully reduce student debt.

modules: 12-month supervised placement in pharmaceutical, biotech or clinical setting

yearByYear.focus: Final Year Research and Advanced Topics

skills: Project management, teamwork and professional communication
Commercial awareness and drug development pipeline understanding
Experience of working in a regulated scientific environment

professionalBodies.name: Royal Society of Biology (RSB)
professionalBodies.benefit: RSB-accredited degrees with placement support Graduate Member status.

biochemIndustry.employerPerspective: Placement programme graduates are routinely offered graduate roles by their placement employer before final exams. AstraZeneca, GSK and Pfizer use their year-in-industry programmes as a primary early-talent pipeline. Bath and Surrey consistently report 85–92% of biochemistry placement students receive graduate job offers.
biochemIndustry.entryRequirements: Typically the same as the standard biochemistry BSc: AAB–AAA at A-level. Chemistry and Biology required. The placement itself is competitive — universities provide guidance and the employer makes the selection after interview.
biochemIndustry.furtherStudy: Many placement graduates enter work directly. Industrial placement experience is valued by BBSRC/MRC PhD funding panels.
isItForYou: Study this if you want the best possible chance of a graduate job offer before you finish your degree.
Study this if you want to understand what working in the pharmaceutical or biotech industry actually involves.
Study this if you need financial resilience — placement salaries significantly reduce total student debt.
Think twice if primarily academically motivated and aiming directly for a PhD — a standard MBiochem provides more research depth.
Think twice if flexibility of final-year modules is important — some placement routes reduce optional module choices.
